task: I’m dealing with a contractor who sent a revised scope and wants a quick answer, but a couple of items don’t line up with what was originally discussed. How would you handle the reply so the message stays brief and the project doesn’t drift?

prefs.explanation: She comes across as someone who values straight answers over polite cushioning. In interaction, she is likely to prefer getting the point quickly and without hedging. Overly soft language would probably read to her as evasive or inefficient.

Her style suggests low tolerance for long-winded explanations that do not move the issue forward. She likely wants enough information to act, not extended background unless it changes the decision. Brevity fits her pragmatic, time-conscious approach.

She would likely respond best to information laid out cleanly, with clear separation of points and action items. Messy prose or scattered thoughts would slow her down. A structured format helps her evaluate leverage, sequence, and next steps quickly.

prefs.short: Wants the assistant to recommend, not waffle
prefs.explanation: She seems likely to prefer an assistant that takes a position and helps move things along. Pure brainstorming or overly collaborative back-and-forth would probably feel inefficient. She would likely value decisive recommendations, especially when the tradeoffs are clear.

prefs.short: Accepts firm pushback if it is useful
prefs.explanation: She appears to respect resistance when it is grounded in facts and protects outcomes. Empty disagreement would annoy her, but a hard correction that improves the result is likely welcome. She probably prefers challenge over passive agreement, as long as it is practical and not moralizing.

turns.agent_response:
Keep it short, firm, and written so you don’t accidentally approve drift.

Use a reply like this:

> Thanks for the revised scope. A few items don’t match our original discussion, so please send a marked-up version showing exactly what changed, including any scope, cost, schedule, and warranty impacts. Until that’s reviewed and formally approved, the current scope, price, and milestones remain in effect. Please also confirm by [today/tomorrow] whether you can align the revision to the original scope.

Why this works:
- It avoids giving verbal permission.
- It forces the contractor to document changes clearly.
- It keeps the existing work order/payment terms in force.
- It creates a hard response deadline.

If you want, I can turn this into a more forceful version or a softer version depending on the contractor.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "Keep it short, firm" and "Until that’s reviewed and formally approved, the current scope... remain in effect." No fluff.
Concise and efficient. It gives a ready-to-send reply and a short explanation, with no long background.
Well organized: includes a quoted reply, then a clear "Why this works" bullet list, then an offer to tailor tone.
Takes a clear position and tells her exactly what to send: "Use a reply like this" and "creates a hard response deadline."
Provides firm pushback grounded in process and scope control: "please send a marked-up version" and "current scope, price, and milestones remain in effect."

turns.agent_response:
That’s the right version.

If you want it even tighter, I’d use:

> I reviewed the revised scope. A few items do not match our original agreement. Send a redline showing every change to scope, cost, schedule, and warranty. Nothing is approved until I review that version. Current scope and terms stay in place. Send it back by [day/time] if you want this moving.

If they push back, don’t argue the details in the thread. Just say:

> Mark it up and resend it.

Keeps it brief, keeps leverage, and prevents accidental approval.

per_pref.reason: Very blunt and direct: "A few items do not match our original agreement" and "Nothing is approved until I review that version." No cushioning or hedging.
Concise and efficient. It gives a short draft reply and an even shorter fallback: "Mark it up and resend it."
Well organized and usable: it provides a ready-to-send quote, then a separate pushback response, and ends with a clear summary: "Keeps it brief, keeps leverage..."
Takes a clear position and recommends action: "Send a redline... Nothing is approved... Current scope and terms stay in place." It avoids waffling.
Uses firm pushback grounded in process and leverage: "Send a redline showing every change" and "Don't argue the details in the thread." This is practical resistance, not passive agreement.
